methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $2.0B last post money.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Chips & Semiconductors. Range: $500M - $5.0B.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Semiconductors P/S median is 22.3x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

private.name: Etched
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series A), post-money $5B

private.name: Substrate
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series A), post-money $1B+

private.name: Rain AI
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series A), post-money $500M

data.narrative:
d-Matrix is a Series A semiconductor company founded by Sid Sheth, currently valued at $2.0B post-money on $450M raised. The valuation analysis draws from a comp universe of 3 public semiconductor peers (ALAB, NVDA, AMD) and 8 private peers, with particular focus on 3 same-sector, same-stage companies: Etched ($5.0B), Substrate ($1.0B), and Rain AI ($500M).

Two methods proved applicable. Stage-Progression analysis, anchored to the $2.0B last post-money valuation and applying a standard 2.0x Series A step-up multiplier, implies a forward valuation of $4.0B. Peer-Median analysis across the three same-stage semiconductor comparables yields a median valuation of $1.0B, with a range spanning $500M to $5.0B. Public-Comp Anchoring was not applicable given the absence of disclosed revenue figures for the target, though the public semiconductor universe trades at a 22.3x median P/S. 

The triangulated valuation range is $850M to $4.6B, with a median of $2.5B. The current $2.0B valuation sits near the lower-median portion of this range, approximately aligned with peer benchmarks but below the stage-progression step-up. Key uncertainties include the wide dispersion of peer valuations in the semiconductor frontier-tech space and the absence of revenue-based anchoring, both of which limit conviction in the upper end of the range.
